doomsday_clock.context: Closest setting in the Clock’s history. Main drivers: nuclear risk (including the end of key arms limits), climate pressure, and risky new technologies.

overall_summary.framing: This page is built for Australia first, in plain language. Two big pressures matter: nuclear risk after major arms limits expired, and a strengthening El Niño that official forecasts say is likely to become very strong by late 2026. For Australia that often means hotter, drier weather and a harder bushfire season. Always trust BOM and your state emergency service over any website.
overall_summary.cumulative_context: Quiet news days do not cancel a multi-season El Niño or structural nuclear risk. Local prep still starts with heat, fire, water, and official alerts.

categories.name: Seismic Activity
categories.metrics: Several very large quakes (magnitude 7+) in the world in early–mid 2026, including the Philippines.
categories.cumulative_context: Big quakes happened overseas this year. Australia feels fewer giants, but buildings still need to be ready.
categories.conservative_reading: An active year overseas does not mean Australia’s risk suddenly changed. Local readiness still matters.
categories.prep_priority: Know drop-cover-hold. Keep water and basic supplies.
categories.discrepancies: Magnitudes usually agree; damage reports lag.

categories.name: Volcanic Activity
categories.metrics: Several volcanoes on higher alert worldwide; NZ submarine activity caused temporary tsunami warnings in July 2026.
categories.cumulative_context: Australia is not a major volcano country. Regional ash can still disrupt flights.
categories.conservative_reading: Follow official alerts if ash or tsunami warnings appear in the region.
categories.prep_priority: If regional ash affects flights or air, follow airline and health advice.

recent_events.description: Submarine volcano activity near New Zealand; tsunami warnings later cancelled

categories.discrepancies: Alert timing can differ by agency.

categories.name: Solar & Space Weather
categories.metrics: January 2026: one of the strongest solar radiation storms in decades, plus a major geomagnetic storm.
categories.cumulative_context: The Sun has been active. Big storms can affect satellites and, rarely, power systems.
categories.conservative_reading: Treat strong solar storms as real operational risks, not just pretty auroras.
categories.prep_priority: Most people need no special action. In rare big storms, GPS or radio can glitch.

recent_events.description: Major solar radiation and geomagnetic storm
recent_events.impact: Satellite and polar aviation risk; wide auroras

categories.discrepancies: Storm grades are standard; specific tech damage is often under-reported.

categories.name: Ocean Temperatures & Marine Heatwaves
categories.metrics: Official Jul 2026 forecast: about 97% chance this El Niño lasts into early 2027; about 81% chance of a very strong peak late 2026 — among the strongest since 1950.
categories.cumulative_context: This is a major issue for Australia. Strong El Niño years often mean hotter, drier weather and harder fire seasons. Some computer models that track deep ocean heat look even more severe than soft media summaries.
categories.conservative_reading: For Australia, plan as if a strong multi-season El Niño is coming. Softer headlines can lag the official numbers.
categories.prep_priority: Watch BOM climate outlooks. Plan early for heat and fire if you farm, manage water, or live near bush.

recent_events.description: Official forecast: high chance of a very strong El Niño by late 2026
recent_events.impact: Raises odds of hotter, drier conditions for many Australian regions

recent_events.description: Extreme Australian heat (reports near 50°C in places)
recent_events.impact: Real stress on people, power, and fire risk

categories.sources_compared: NOAA/CPC, climate model groups, BOM context
categories.discrepancies: Some models are more severe than others. The official very-strong probability is not fringe.

categories.name: Sea Level Rise & Coastal Extremes
categories.metrics: Seas continue rising by a few millimetres a year on average.
categories.cumulative_context: The everyday effect is more minor flooding on high tides, not a single movie-style flood.
categories.conservative_reading: Adaptation and insurance matter more than panic.
categories.prep_priority: If you are coastal, know local flood maps and insurance gaps.

recent_events.description: High-tide minor flooding in low-lying coasts (global pattern)

categories.name: Weather Extremes, Drought & Heat Stress
categories.metrics: Early 2026: extreme heat in Australia and other Southern Hemisphere regions.
categories.cumulative_context: Heat is already hurting people. A strong El Niño raises the odds of more heat and dry weather into 2027.
categories.conservative_reading: Plan for more heat and dry spells — especially where BOM outlooks point that way.
categories.prep_priority: Have a heat plan: cool room, water, check on neighbours. Follow BOM heatwave warnings.

recent_events.description: Southern Hemisphere extreme heat including Australia

categories.discrepancies: Local records are firm; one event is never the whole climate story.

categories.name: Wildfire Activity & Air Quality
categories.metrics: Severe fires in several countries in early 2026; Australia already faced extreme heat and fire weather.
categories.cumulative_context: Dry fuels and hot weather raise bushfire danger. Smoke can harm people far from the flames.
categories.conservative_reading: Treat fire season readiness as normal work in fire country.
categories.prep_priority: Near bush: written leave-early plan, go-bag, P2 masks for smoke. Use your state fire app.

recent_events.description: Severe fires and extreme heat weather in the Southern Hemisphere, including Australian fire-weather stress
recent_events.impact: Danger to communities and air quality

categories.discrepancies: Burned-area totals change through the season.

categories.name: Military Conflict & Escalation Risks
categories.metrics: Major wars ongoing; nuclear threats have been used in politics for years.
categories.cumulative_context: Overseas wars can still hit fuel and food prices in Australia.
categories.conservative_reading: Conflict risk is not only local to the battlefield.
categories.prep_priority: Expect possible price swings. Stick to reputable news.

recent_events.description: Ongoing major wars with global economic effects

categories.discrepancies: Analysts disagree on escalation odds; the fighting itself is documented.

categories.name: Near-Earth Objects (NEOs)
categories.metrics: No serious near-term asteroid impact on public watch lists.
categories.conservative_reading: No public action needed for impact risk now.
categories.prep_priority: No action needed for impact risk right now.

recent_events.description: Routine small space-rock flybys at safe distances

categories.name: Geomagnetic Field Stability & Core Dynamics
categories.metrics: Earth’s magnetic field is slowly weakening and the magnetic poles keep moving.
categories.cumulative_context: This is not an imminent pole-flip disaster. It mainly affects satellites and power planning over many years.
categories.conservative_reading: Slow infrastructure issue, not a household emergency.

recent_events.description: Continued magnetic pole movement and South Atlantic weak-zone growth

categories.discrepancies: Scientists agree the field is changing; they do not predict an imminent full flip.

categories.name: Infrastructure & Financial Strain
categories.metrics: Stacked pressure from climate, conflict, and tech disruption raises costs for ordinary life.
categories.cumulative_context: When heat, fire, and global shocks stack up, food, power, and insurance get harder.
categories.conservative_reading: Buffers beat predicting the next single headline.
categories.prep_priority: If you can, keep a small buffer of water, food, medicine, and cash.

recent_events.description: Stacked heat, fire, and climate-pressure costs

categories.discrepancies: Official numbers lag what households feel.

categories.name: Nuclear Risk
categories.metrics: Doomsday Clock at 85 seconds. Last major US–Russia arms-limit treaty expired in February 2026 with no replacement.
categories.cumulative_context: This is the main reason the Clock is so close to midnight. Ordinary prep cannot solve it.
categories.conservative_reading: Nuclear risk is the leading global catastrophe pathway on this page. Politics and arms control are the real levers.
categories.prep_priority: You cannot fully prep for nuclear war. Support serious arms-control politics. Ignore social-media nuclear rumours.

recent_events.description: New START arms treaty expires with no replacement
recent_events.impact: No verified limits between the two largest nuclear arsenals

categories.sources_compared: Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists and arms-control research
categories.discrepancies: Experts disagree on exact odds of use; they agree structural risk is high.
